name: Plants & Trees
description: Botanical world — trees, flowers, and plant facts

questions.q: Cricket bats are traditionally made from the wood of which tree?

source.notes: Specifically the 'cricket-bat willow' (Salix alba var. caerulea).

questions.q: Tequila is distilled from the fermented juice of which plant?

source.notes: By Mexican law, must be made primarily from blue Weber agave grown in designated regions.

questions.q: Rum is made by fermenting and distilling which plant?

source.notes: Either molasses or fresh sugar-cane juice, depending on style (e.g., rhum agricole uses fresh juice).

questions.q: The spice saffron is harvested from the stigmas of which flower?

source.notes: Crocus sativus; one of the most expensive spices by weight.

questions.q: Which flower is the national flower of Bulgaria, a major producer of its essential oil?

source.notes: The Rose Valley near Kazanlak supplies much of the world's rose attar.

questions.q: Which grain is the primary ingredient in most beers?

source.notes: Barley is malted — germinated and dried — to provide fermentable sugars.

questions.q: Japan is famed in spring for the blossoms of which tree?

source.notes: Known as sakura; the national flower of Japan along with the chrysanthemum.

questions.q: The oak is traditionally the national tree of which country, where 'the Royal Oak' refers to the tree that once hid Charles II?

source.notes: Charles II hid in the Boscobel Oak after the Battle of Worcester in 1651.

questions.q: A cedar tree appears at the center of which country's national flag?

source.notes: The cedar of Lebanon (Cedrus libani) is a national symbol.

questions.q: Aspirin's active ingredient, salicylic acid, was originally derived from the bark of which tree?

source.notes: Known since ancient times; Bayer commercialized acetylsalicylic acid as Aspirin in 1899.

questions.q: The art of cultivating miniature trees in pots is most strongly associated with which country?

source.notes: The practice is called 'bonsai' in Japan; derived from the older Chinese tradition of penjing.

questions.q: The iconic baobab tree, which has a swollen trunk used to store water, is most strongly associated with which African island and country?

source.notes: Six of the nine recognized baobab species are endemic to Madagascar.

questions.q: The carnivorous Venus flytrap is native to a small region of which U.S. area?

source.notes: Native only to subtropical wetlands within about a 60-mile radius of Wilmington, North Carolina.

questions.q: An olive branch is most universally recognized as a symbol of what?

source.notes: Goes back to ancient Greece and is referenced in Genesis (the dove after the flood).

questions.q: The sunflower is the national flower of which country?

source.notes: Ukraine is one of the world's largest producers of sunflower seeds and oil.

questions.q: The tulip is the national flower of which country, from where tulip mania spread to Europe in the 1600s?

source.notes: Tulips were cultivated in the Ottoman Empire before being introduced to the Netherlands.

questions.q: The thistle is the national emblem of which country in the United Kingdom?

source.notes: Legend attributes its adoption to a Norse invader stepping on one and alerting Scottish defenders.

questions.q: The Latin genus name 'Acer' refers to which group of trees?

source.notes: Includes the sugar maple (Acer saccharum), which yields maple syrup.

questions.q: The unusually bent pine trees of the Crooked Forest (Krzywy Las) are located in which country?

source.notes: Located near Gryfino in north-western Poland; about 400 J-shaped pines.

questions.q: The antimalarial drug quinine was originally extracted from the bark of which tree?

source.notes: Cinchona is native to the Andes; sometimes called the 'fever tree'.

questions.q: The beech is the national tree of which Scandinavian country?

source.notes: The European beech (Fagus sylvatica) was adopted as Denmark's national tree in 1989.

questions.q: In Buddhism and Hinduism, the lotus flower most notably symbolizes what?

source.notes: Because the flower rises unstained from muddy water, it symbolizes purity and enlightenment.
